message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To identify factors predicting symptomatic laboratory-positive SARS-COV-2 reinfection.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We conducted a nationwide retrospective cohort study and data from 99,993 confirmed cases of COVID-19 were analyzed.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The overall risk of reinfection (28 or more elapsed days between both episodes onset) was 0.21%, and older subjects and those with mild primary disease were at reduced risk of reinfection. Healthcare workers and immunosuppressed or renal patients had at greater risk of SARS-COV-2 reinfection.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>If replicated in other populations, these results may be useful to prioritize efforts focusing on the reduction of SARS-COV-2 spread and the related burden.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Predictors of symptomatic laboratory-confirmed SARS-COV-2 reinfection

reference.unstructured: Tomassini S , Kotecha D , Bird PW , Folwell A , Biju S , Tang JW : Setting the criteria for SARS-CoV-2 reinfection - six possible cases. J Infect 2020.
